participants.reasoning: The extracted criteria differ from the official study: it states age 65+ with broader fall-risk criteria and different exclusions, whereas the official participants were aged ≥70 with falls in the past 6 months or fear of falling, and excluded independent ambulation and cognitive impairment.
participants.extracted: Community-dwelling adults aged 65 years and older who were at high risk of falls and were recruited through participating general practices in the greater Munich and Nuremberg areas of Bavaria, Germany. Eligibility required at least one fall in the previous 12 months, fear of falling, prolonged chair-stand time (>10 seconds), prolonged Timed Up and Go time (>10 seconds), balance impairment on the Guralnik short physical performance battery, or self-reported subjective balance deficits. Individuals not living independently, or those with physical or mental restrictions that would prevent participation in an exercise program or completion of fall-risk assessments, were excluded.
participants.official:
Country: Germany
n = 122
Female: 122 (43.6%)
Mean age (years): 76.1 (SD 4.1)
Inclusion criteria: aged ≥ 70 years, having fallen in the past 6 months or FoF, provided signed informed consent, and completed baseline assessment
Exclusion criteria: unable to ambulate independently, cognitive impairment (DSST score < 25)

interventions.reasoning: The extracted intervention matches the official description in overall format, duration, group delivery, exercise components, fall-risk education, and fear-of-falling CBT elements, with only minor wording differences and generalization.
interventions.extracted: The intervention was a 16-week multimodal fall-prevention program delivered in small groups once weekly for 60 minutes, combined with individualized home exercises and physical-activity recommendations. It included progressive strength training, flexibility exercises, challenging balance training, gait and motor coordination training, endurance training, and practice of getting up from the floor. The program also incorporated educational components on fall risk reduction and a cognitive-behavioral “fear of falling” module adapted from the Matter of Balance approach, aimed at increasing self-efficacy and reducing fear-related avoidance of activities. Sessions were led by trained physiotherapists or sport scientists and were designed for community-dwelling older adults at high risk of falls.
interventions.official:
CBT with exercise
Group‐based (< 15 participants), 2 × 1‐hour sessions per week, over 16 weeks, at an institution (Institute of Sports Sciences, Erlangen, Germany)
Delivered by "fall prevention instructors" (trained by physiotherapist or university‐trained sport scientist)
Arm 1: Strength and Balance group (n = 63); progressive exercises for upper and lower body strength (standing weight‐bearing exercises with dumbbells and challenging balance exercises), motor co‐ordination training, ball games

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include several measures not listed in the official outcomes (e.g., FES-I, EuroQol, pain-related injury outcomes, and SPPB components), while the official list includes outcomes not captured in the extraction such as ABC, DSST, perceived consequences of falling, days walking per week, and walking speed.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure falls as the primary outcome, defined as the number of falls per individual over a 12-month follow-up period, and also the risk of falling (proportion with at least one fall during follow-up). Falls were recorded throughout the full 24-month study period using a daily fall diary/calendar, with details of each fall obtained by structured telephone interview. Secondary outcomes included incidence and frequency of fall-related injuries, balance, strength, and mobility, assessed at baseline (month 0), 4 months, 12 months, and 24 months using the Timed Up and Go test, chair-stand-up test, and balance tests from the Short Physical Performance Battery (Romberg, tandem stand, semi-tandem stand). Fear of falling was assessed at baseline, 4, 12, and 24 months using the German version of the Falls Efficacy Scale-International (FES-I). Quality of life was assessed at the same time points using the EuroQol questionnaire, and overall health status was rated on a visual analogue scale (0-100). Physical activity was assessed at baseline, 4, 12, and 24 months using the 7-Day Physical Active Recall (PAR).
outcomes.official: ABC; 2 subscales of the perceived Consequences of Falling scale (Loss of Functional Independence, Damage to Identity); DSST; Single‐item FoF (yes/no); falls; number of days walking per week; TUG; modified Romberg Test; chair rise test; self‐selected normal and fast walking speed over a 10‐m distance
